There are so many different types of dog treats out there, it can be tough to know where to start! Some other options you might want to try are freeze-dried meat treats, which are usually made from a single ingredient (like chicken or beef) and are packed with flavor. You could also try making your own treats at home using a simple recipe like mixing together some mashed sweet potato and a little bit of cooked chicken. Or, you could try giving her some healthy human foods as treats, like small pieces of cooked carrot or a little bit of cheese. Just be sure to check with your vet first to make sure any new treats you give her are safe and appropriate for her specific needs.
Are there any faux pas with making dog treats? Say, like adding chocolate to the mix ?

There are definitely some ingredients that you'll want to avoid when making treats for your dog. Chocolate is one of them, as it can be toxic to dogs and can cause symptoms like vomiting, diarrhea, and tremors. Other ingredients to avoid include things like grapes and raisins, which can also be toxic to dogs, as well as onions and garlic, which can cause anemia. You'll also want to be careful about giving your dog treats that are high in fat or sugar, as these can contribute to health problems like obesity and dental issues. Stick to using simple, healthy ingredients, and you should be good to go!
Shes a Dachshund so she is a bit fat and might potentially develop diabetes. Any treats that might keep her lean?

If your Dachshund is at risk for developing diabetes or is overweight, it's especially important to be mindful of the treats you give her. Look for low-fat, low-sugar treats that are made with healthy ingredients like whole grains, fruits, and vegetables. You might also want to try giving her smaller amounts of treats more frequently, rather than a few larger treats throughout the day. Another option could be to use healthy "people food" as treats, like small pieces of cooked carrot or a slice of apple. Just be sure to check with your vet before making any changes to your dog's diet or treat routine, to make sure you're meeting her specific needs.
Maybe some daily exercise might be essential as well. Would you happen to know how much exercise a dog of her breed might need per day?

The amount of exercise your Dachshund needs will depend on a variety of factors, including her age, size, and overall health. In general, Dachshunds are energetic little dogs that need plenty of daily exercise to stay happy and healthy. Aim to give your Dachshund at least 30 minutes of exercise per day, with a mix of walks, runs, and playtime. This could include activities like going for a walk around the neighborhood, playing fetch in the park, or just running around in the backyard. Just be sure to pay attention to your dog's energy levels and needs, and adjust the amount of exercise accordingly. If you're not sure how much exercise your Dachshund needs, your vet can help you come up with a plan that's right for her.

But at the same time, it's also important to make decisions that are in the best interest of your business. If switching to a different logistics company could improve your margins, it might be worth considering. It's always a good idea to explore all your options before making a decision.

I think I'm decided. I will look to switch logistics of our main product to a different, more efficient company. Sure thing! Here's one way you could approach it:"Thank you for your continued support and loyalty to our company. We value our relationship with you and have always appreciated the competitive prices and reliable service you've provided.However, we've been facing some challenges with our margins and have made the difficult decision to switch the logistics of our main product to a different company. We hope you understand our need to make this decision and we look forward to continuing to work with you in the future."This message expresses your appreciation for the company's past support, while also being clear and firm about the decision you've made. It also leaves the door open for continuing the relationship in some capacity.
